item.title: Car lost control on Avenida da Amizade and collided with a car and a bus
item.description:
Four residents were injured and required treatment in hospital following a collision between three vehicles, on Wednesday, on Avenida da Amizade. A seven-seater car reportedly lost control after overtaking on a curve and, after crossing the road, collided with another car and a public bus, according to the preliminary investigation by police authorities. Several water barriers were destroyed in the accident, with the front part of the three vehicles being heavily damaged.
Avenida da Amizade was once again the site of a serious road accident on Wednesday night which, this time, involved two seven-seater cars and a public bus. The road collision happened around 7:30 pm near Largo do Terminal Marítimo and was caused after a light vehicle lost control after overtaking on a curve. The accident caused four injuries, including three drivers and a bus passenger, all residents of Macau, aged between 35 and 61 years. Among them, the driver of one of the private vehicles, who was temporarily trapped in the vehicle after the collision, suffered a broken leg, while the others suffered chest bruises. The injured were in stable condition when they were taken to the hospital for medical treatment. The accident site was chaotic, with vehicles seriously damaged, which caused traffic in the area to become restricted, with many people on the outskirts observing the situation. According to preliminary investigations by the authorities, the incident was caused by a white seven-seater car, which was traveling in the direction of Rua dos Pescadores towards Avenida Dr. Sun Yat-Sen. When passing a curve and approaching the water barriers, the car lost control after overtaking, crossing the road in the opposite direction and colliding with the water barriers, subsequently colliding with another seven-seater car. Due to the force of the collision, the cars also hit a public bus that was traveling behind, causing a chain accident. Wong Chi Wang, head of the Traffic Police Division of the Public Security Police Corps, attended a press conference at the scene after the accident, and stated that the bus driver and the driver of the white car passed the alcohol test, and the driver of the other car was expected to undergo the test later. “It is suspected that the accident involved reckless overtaking on the curve, as well as excessive speed on the curve”, highlighted the police officer, adding that he will monitor the case against the driver in question. The CPSP added that, in this case, 14 agents were mobilized and the section of road in question was closed to begin relief operations in conjunction with firefighters. The Fire Department (CB) sent four ambulances and two emergency vehicles, with a total of 26 firefighters deployed to the scene. At the press conference, Ng Chon Leong, first chief of the CB, pointed out that the driver of the affected car had his legs trapped due to the damage and deformations to the vehicle, so firefighters had to use specialized tools to cut the steering wheel, using support equipment to remove it from the scene. The entire process took almost 40 minutes. Several videos and images circulating on the Internet show that Avenida da Amizade was covered in water and vehicle debris, as well as fragments of the water barrier scattered across the ground, with the front part of the two cars being seriously damaged, and the front part of the bus being dented and the windshield broken. In the on-board camera video of the moment of the accident, shared on social media, it is seen that the white seven-seater car appeared at high speed in the left lane and then changed to the right lane, colliding head-on with the water barriers, as well as vehicles in the opposite direction. Due to the accident, the section of Avenida da Amizade in the area of ​​the incident had to be temporarily closed. At 8:45 pm, after the three vehicles involved in the accident had all been removed from the scene, traffic on this section was partially reopened.

item.title: Lack of children in Hong Kong leaves 15 primaries at risk of closing their doors
item.description:
At least 15 primary schools are at risk of closing in Hong Kong because they have failed to attract a sufficient number of new students, in a context of historic lows in the region's birth rate.
The Secretary for Education, Christine Choi Yuk-lin, warned this Wednesday that more primaries could close and advised educational institutions to seek mergers to keep their doors open. Hong Kong authorities have announced that they will not subsidize 15 primary schools that received fewer than 16 applications for the first year of schooling in the next academic year, 2026/2027. One of them is the Five Districts Business Association School, an institution with 69 years of history, where the current leader of the local government, John Lee Ka-chiu, studied. Christine Choi told public broadcaster RTHK that the former British colony has registered a decreasing number of school-age children over the last ten years, due to the drop in the birth rate. The number of students enrolled in the first year of primary education in 2026-27 decreased by around four thousand compared to the current academic year, added the Secretary for Education. But the worst is yet to come, Choi admitted. "The number could fall even further next year, considering the drop in the birth rate. We need to turn the page today and use new ideas to face the problem", said the leader. “Just planning the next step does not offer us a stable environment,” he added. Hong Kong's Education Department expects the number of six-year-old children to fall from 47,000 in 2025 to 38,300 by 2035. Christine Choi advised institutions with low student enrollment to look into mergers to avoid more closures in the future. “The government has offered several measures to facilitate the recovery of schools in recent years (…) Now there is no going back, and we have to face the problem head on”, said the Secretary for Education. Hong Kong recorded around 31,100 births in 2025, the lowest number ever. In 2023, the Government launched a subsidy of 20 thousand Hong Kong dollars (around 2,150 euros) for new parents, to encourage families to have children, in a program lasting three years. The Executive predicted that the subsidy could help the annual number of births reach 39 thousand, 20% more than in 2022. But the total number of newborns was 33,200 in 2023 and 36,700 in 2024. In September, John Lee announced that, from 2026, the HK$130,000 tax exemption for new parents will be extended from one to two years after birth. Last year, the neighboring Chinese region of Macau also registered 2,871 newborns, the lowest number in almost half a century. In 2025, mainland China recorded 7.92 million births, a new historic low since the founding of the People's Republic of China in 1949. The birth rate also fell to historic lows, at 5.63 per thousand people. Lusa

item.title: European discomfort with Chinese politics
item.description: Europe is divided between the desire for political unity with numerous fissures and the geopolitical challenges posed by an international order in reconfiguration. Regarding the relationship with China, three events marked this disorientation: the EU-China summit on July 24, 2025, the Shanghai Cooperation Organization meeting on August 31 and the military parade in Beijing on the so-called Victory Day. Unsteady on its position regarding the war in Ukraine, Europe finds it extremely difficult to find the most appropriate stance to readjustments in Chinese foreign policy. For decades, Europe looked to China as the “good student” of the market economy, channeling billions of euros into its internal market, in aid for investment, research and development, and the promotion of the rule of law. He did not realize that this aid would not be reciprocated and that Beijing looks at its interests from the point of view of real-politik and not from solidarity drives. This country has changed significantly with the bilateral relationship. Consider the asymmetry in the opening of the respective markets, the trade deficit with Europe in favor of Beijing. In 2024 the trade deficit was 305.8 billion euros, in 2025 320 billion euros, pronouncing a clear trend towards growth. Seeking to protect European interests, the Van Der Leyen Commission abandoned its stance of convergence with China and in 2023 adopted what it called a “discrimination” strategy. This aimed to minimize economic weaknesses and critical dependencies in sectors such as semiconductors, artificial intelligence and biotechnology. At the same time, it has attempted to create mechanisms for risk assessment and protection of sensitive European technologies, particularly those with potential military use, so-called dual-use technologies. At the July 2025 summit, the divergence between the two partners was notable. Displeased with the soaring trade deficit and commercial practices that it called “unfair”, the European Union demanded that Beijing take measures to correct the persistent imbalances. Eight months later, it remains to be seen whether these demands have an effective impact on decision-makers. At the August 2025 Shanghai Cooperation Organization Summit in Tianjin, the subservience of Vladimir Putin, Narenda Mondi and other leaders of the non-Western world to Xi Jinping was noted. Beijing's position of express support for Russian foreign policy constitutes a readjustment, of enormous significance, in the traditional policy of neutrality towards the great powers and the conflicts in which they are involved. I remember the war in Ukraine, the conflict with Venezuela and more recently the American-Israeli operations against Iran, a traditional ally of Beijing and a major supplier of crude oil and other essential raw materials to the Chinese energy sector. China is already Russia's biggest energy buyer, Sino-Russian trade has soared and Beijing exports to Russia semiconductors, drones and “dual” electronic components that Moscow uses in war. The “Comprehensive Strategic Coordination Partnership for a New Era” agreed in May 2025 between the two powers differs little from the traditional alliances of the 20th century, although Beijing is quick to say that it is not an alliance against anyone but a strengthened cooperation partnership. In the military parade on September 3, in Tiananmen, the closeness and affability between the two Russian and Chinese leaders was notable and in some world capitals this harmony and the solemnity of the military parade reminded us of other events that darken the memory of 20th century Europe.

item.title: Law approved restricting choice of lawyers in national security trials
item.description:
The deputies of the Legislative Assembly (AL) yesterday unanimously approved the proposed law of the Macao SAR State Security Defense Commission. The diploma establishes that it is this commission, whose president is the Chief Executive, who decides which lawyers can defend defendants accused of violating the national security law. This commission will also have the power to decide whether trials, in cases of national security, are public or behind closed doors.
Yesterday, the Legislative Assembly (AL) approved the bill for the Macao SAR State Security Defense Commission. At yesterday's plenary meeting, deputies ended up approving all articles of the proposal unanimously. After approval, all deputies presented declarations of vote defending and applauding the law. The proposed law therefore provides that in cases of national security, judicial representatives will have to request special authorization from the judge before intervening in the process. In the application, the lawyer must provide information regarding household members, nationality and connection with organizations or individuals abroad. The judge, in turn, will have to forward the application to the State Security Defense Commission, which then decides whether or not the lawyer can defend the accused, with no complaint or contentious appeal for this decision. The bill does not define the criteria to be used by the Commission to decide whether or not a lawyer is able to defend the accused. Furthermore, the State Security Defense Commission will also have the power to confirm the judge's decision to keep the trial public or to hold it behind closed doors. The body will also monitor risks to national security in different areas, such as education, culture and economy, for example. Au Kam San, former AL deputy, is currently detained awaiting trial in the context of the first – and only so far – case related to national security in the history of the Macao SAR. The Democrat was arrested on July 31 last year for alleged collusion with foreign forces. This trial, which has not yet been scheduled, must be governed by the rules that will be imposed by this diploma.
SECRETARY GUARANTEES THAT DEFENDANTS’ RIGHTS REMAIN PROTECTED
During the debate, Chan Tsz King, Secretary for Security, took the opportunity to reiterate that this proposal aims to “protect the security of the State” and will not “deprive the right of defense” of the defendants. “This regime will not take away the right of defense or the professional qualifications of lawyers”, assured Chan Tsz King. Regarding the possibility of the trial being held behind closed doors, the secretary indicated that the mechanism serves “to implement a barrier to defend national security and to prevent procedural acts from being harmed”. During the discussion, Chan Tsz King also said that the diploma that will regulate the Secretariat of the State Security Defense Commission is almost ready and should come into force alongside the law itself. After approval, the law will come into force the day following its publication in the Official Gazette. Chan Tsz King clarified that the reports that the Government will send annually to the AL on the organization's expenses will only show the total amount of expenses in a non-detailed way so that the commission's work maintains its “confidentiality”. In yesterday's session, the government official also promised to implement an awareness plan about the new law, so that all sectors are better aware of the diploma: “The Government will use different means and contact different associations to provide clarifications”.
Government thanks AL for approving the law
A few minutes after the Macao SAR State Security Defense Commission's proposed law was approved in the LA, the Government released a statement to thank the executive body for approving the law. “The Macao SAR Government sincerely thanks the Legislative Assembly, all sectors of society and all Macau residents for the strong sense of responsibility and deep patriotic feeling demonstrated”, can be read in the press release. The Government emphasizes that “the defense of the country’s sovereignty, security and development interests constitutes the supreme foundation of the principle of ‘one country, two systems’, being a constitutional responsibility that falls to the Macau Special Administrative Region”. The authorities say that, since the drafting, in 2009, of the law on the defense of state security and, subsequently, its review in 2023, until now, “it has followed the evolution of times, adapting to the new circumstances and demands of national security in the new era; and has consolidated the legal foundations to defend national security”. “This journey vividly illustrates the successful implementation of the fundamental principle of ‘Macau governed by patriots’ and embodies the demands of the comprehensive implementation of the general perspective of national security”, it reads. According to the Government, the law now approved “fully” implements the provisions set out in the law relating to the defense of State security and “strengthens the top structure of the national security defense system”. The text of this proposed law “fully demonstrates the organic unity between the full governance power of the Central Government and the high degree of autonomy of the Macao SAR”, says the Government, adding that the diploma “is favorable to the supervision, guidance, coordination and support, on the part of the advisors for national security matters to the Macao SAR in the development of work to defend national security and has an important meaning in ensuring that these works always proceed in the correct direction”.
AAM and Gabriel Tong express support for the diploma
The Macau Lawyers Association (AAM) and Gabriel Tong, director of the Faculty of Law of the University of Macau (UM), applauded the approval of the Macao SAR State Security Defense Commission law [see page 13]. AAM “firmly defends and supports” the legislative initiative and understands that the regime “does not deprive interested parties of the right to defense, nor does it affect the professional qualifications of lawyers”. The law “contributes to better safeguarding the country's sovereignty, security and development interests” and, on the other hand, “it also allows for better adaptation to the new demands of practicing the principle of ‘one country, two systems'”, argues the association led by Vong Hin Fai. Gabriel Tong begins by pointing out that “the international strategic panorama is undergoing profound adjustments and internal and external security risks are interconnected”. Therefore, he argues, “only through the full implementation of the general perspective of national security is it possible to effectively defend national security and safeguard Macau's prosperous, stable and lasting development situation”. The director of the UM Faculty of Law considers that, after the law comes into force, “the Macao SAR will have a more complete and solid system and mechanisms for the defense of national security”.

item.title: Falling into an oily swamp? The US and Israeli war against Iran
item.description:
The ongoing war between the US and Israel against Iran is revealing a phenomenon in which both the US and Israel are falling into an oily quagmire, in which Iran remains militarily resilient and politically defiant. Unless both parties sit down at the table and negotiate a temporary solution, with or without any intermediary, or unless one party stops attacking the other, the war will likely be prolonged, with tremendous consequences for the world economy. Iran is not Venezuela, where the US attempt to bring about regime change can go smoothly. Venezuela is a much weaker country than Iran, in geopolitical and military terms. The arrest of Nicolás Maduro led to a relatively easy victory for the USA, which managed to guarantee the pro-American orientation of the “new” government led by Delcy Rodríguez. Venezuela, located in the US's backyard, became a de facto US protectorate to promote the American national interest of influencing its oil production and export. Iran is strategically located between Central Asia and the Middle East, and its military ability to block or disrupt maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz is undoubtedly an asset in the war against the US-Israel alliance. Although US forces have reportedly sunk sixteen Iranian mine ships near the Strait of Hormuz (CNBC, March 11, 2026), Iran has resumed laying mines in the Strait, where ships carrying twenty percent of the world's oil once passed (New York Times, March 13, 2026). Geopolitically, Iran has the ability to disrupt or compromise oil supplies to various parts of the world, forcing the US and Israeli side to consider concessions sooner or later, although US and Israeli military power appears to be dominant. Unlike Venezuela, Iran's new president, Mojtaba Khamenei, issued his first statement after taking over from his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in intense airstrikes launched by the US and Israel at the start of the war. Mojtaba Khamenei insisted on the need for Iranian unity, at the same time as he promised to block the Strait of Hormuz to put pressure on Iran's enemies. As such, it is decided to launch a war of attrition. This war of attrition will likely test the US and Israeli military's ability to deploy missile interceptors, which, according to some reports, will become inadequate over time, especially in the face of a large number of military drones deployed by Iran. The Iranian side established three conditions to end the war: recognition of its rights, payment of compensation and guarantees against future aggression by the US and Israel. The second condition appears to be very difficult to achieve as long as the other side considers war “necessary” to punish Iran during the difficult and fruitless negotiation process on the enriched uranium issue. The first condition, namely the recognition of Iran's rights, will have to be negotiated with considerable difficulty at the table, even if both parties are willing to return to dialogue. According to the Arms Control Association (March 11, 2026), the crucial meeting between the US side and the Iranian side took place on February 28, when US special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner met with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi in Geneva. The meeting was part of the third round of negotiations under the mediation of Oman, whose Minister of Foreign Affairs, Badr bin Hamad Al Busaidi, considered that the North American and Iranian sides had made progress and that both agreed to meet again on March 2 to discuss technical details. However, on February 29, the US and Israel militarily attacked Iran. The Arms Control Association speculated that Witkoff's "lack of knowledge and misinterpretation of Iran's nuclear positions and program throughout the process likely influenced [President Donald] Trump's assessment that negotiations were not moving forward and that Iran was not negotiating seriously" (See: US negotiators were ill-prepared for serious nuclear negotiations with Iran | Arms Control Association). It is not known how the US negotiating team briefed President Trump, but a lack of trust persisted between the US and Iran and led to the outbreak of war. The other main factor is perception. If the US realized that the Iranian side was “not taking negotiations seriously”, war became, without a doubt, inevitable. Three main factors can shorten the duration of the ongoing war. The first is the upcoming November midterm elections in the US. It is highly likely that the war will not last until October, as this would plunge American voters into a wave of anti-Republicanism – a scenario that should be avoided by President Trump and his closest advisors. On March 6, the House of Representatives failed to pass a war powers resolution that would have required the Trump administration to request congressional approval before proceeding with U.S. military action against Iran. Similarly, in the Senate, members rejected by 53 votes to 47 a measure that sought to condemn President Trump's decision to attack Iran without requesting congressional approval. The dominant view in Congress was that the US-Israeli conflict with Iran would be limited, reflecting the majority view that the war would be short-lived. The second factor is President Trump's intention, recalling his previous comment about the duration of the war. He stated, on March 2, that the war with Iran would last between four and five weeks, but that it could last “much longer”. From an analytical perspective, it appears that the United States anticipates that conflicts will end after the elimination of critical targets with missiles and enriched uranium; however, the duration of hostilities may be prolonged – a scenario that depends on the evolution of subsequent events. The third factor is the enormous risk to oil supplies and prices. A prolonged war will increase global suffering. US global leadership will also be challenged and severely criticized by more countries around the world. As such, it is in the interests of the US and Israel to end the war and return to the negotiating table as quickly as possible. It is clear that Iran appears to be defiant at the moment and is reluctant to return to the negotiating table.
Despite the prevailing opinion among members of Congress that the US-Israeli war against Iran will likely be short-lived, there are several factors that could prolong the duration of the war. The first is the difficulty on the part of the US and Israel in fulfilling the three conditions established by the Iranian side, especially the payment of war damages caused to Iran and guarantees against future aggression. “Guarantees” against future aggression will have to be defined by the Iranian side and then negotiated by both parties in a dynamic of mutual concessions – a very difficult process, at least in the short term. The second is the Iranian strategy of launching its missile counterattack against US military bases in different countries, including the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Iraq and Cyprus. The dangers of a military contagion effect in other countries have already been felt, leaving only the hope that the affected countries will not retaliate against Iran, although the Iranian side has stated that the target of the attack is the USA and not its friendly neighbors.
The third is the relationship between Iran and Israel. Both sides have become militarily antagonistic, with Iranian missiles being launched at Israeli bases. Even if a temporary ceasefire is achieved, there will be no guarantee that the Israeli and Iranian sides will restrain themselves in terms of military action against each other. Compounding the problem are ongoing conflicts between Israel and Iranian-allied actors such as Hezbollah and the Houthis. Although Israel appears to contain Hezbollah militarily, there is no guarantee that Iran's allies will not retaliate against Israel in the long term. The fourth is the legacy of the US attack on Iran, which has already caused the deaths of 170 primary school children – an event that fuels even stronger anti-American sentiment in the psyche of Iranians. It was reported that a deadly Tomahawk missile attack, which used outdated data, could have hit an Iranian elementary school that was located near an Iranian military base – an unfortunately tragic development in the US war against Iran (New York Times, March 11, 2026). On March 1, UNESCC expressed its deep concern about the impact of military actions on both sides, highlighting that “attacks against educational institutions endanger students and teachers and undermine the right to education” (UN News, March 1, 2026). UN Secretary-General António Guterres condemned Iran's military strikes and retaliatory attacks that hit several countries in the Middle East. However, once bloodshed is seen, especially among child victims, permanent hatred quickly develops, and military wounds cannot be easily healed. The result will be endless conflicts in the Middle East, with Iran and its allies targeting Israel and the US. Reducing the duration of war requires urgent self-restraint from all parties – a difficult scenario in the short term. One factor preventing the escalation and deterioration of the US and Israeli war against Iran is non-military intervention by third parties, or peaceful mediation by third parties. The US and Israeli attack on Iran appears to alarm North Korea, which condemned the US action and sided with the new Iranian president. Before the US and Israel's conflict with Iran, North Korea called on the US to recognize its status as a nuclear power without preconditions, but President Trump responded that there should be no conditions. Upon seeing US military action against Iran, the North Korean leadership is naturally alarmed and supports Iran. More significantly, the US and Israeli war against Iran has already convinced North Korea that it must develop and possess nuclear weapons to protect itself against the US-South Korea-Japan military alliance in Northeast Asia. It seems unlikely that North Korea will send troops to help Iran, unlike the war between Russia and Ukraine, but it is likely that it could support Iran in terms of military logistics and supplies. A fundamental challenge to world peace is the deep distrust between the USA and some militarily emerging countries, namely Iran, when these nations have the almost total capacity to develop nuclear weapons using highly enriched uranium. If the US and Israel perceive Iran's enriched uranium development process as an existential threat, the joint pre-emptive strike against Iran, from a strategic point of view, is understandable. However, the corollary is that these emerging military countries will continue to see the development of nuclear weapons as a means of self-protection to ward off any existential threats, regardless of whether the threats come from the US, its allies or its enemies. As such, world peace is threatened by perceptions of existential threats between different nations in their uranium enrichment process and their almost total capacity to develop nuclear weapons. Although the International Atomic Energy Agency plays a crucial role in preventing the proliferation of nuclear weapons through independent verification of States' compliance with nuclear non-proliferation commitments, difficulties in implementing related agreements and deep distrust among States appear to be the most controversial issues that endanger world peace. On March 7, President Trump demanded Iran's "unconditional surrender" – a difficult scenario to achieve, given the three peace conditions subsequently presented by Iran. A closer look at how Imperial Japan "surrendered" to the US and its allies on August 15, 1945 reveals that the Japanese emperor at the time used the term "cessation of war" rather than "unconditional surrender." Japan had no choice but to “put an end to the war” at that time, due to the immense impacts of the atomic bombs, one on Hiroshima and the other on Nagasaki. The current US-Israeli war against Iran is limited, without a powerful and lethal attack that could easily bring Iran to its knees before the US-Israeli alliance. Compounding the difficulties of regime change in Iran is the fact that its internal dissident movement remains relatively weak, although it has been reported that 12,000 people or more may have died in recent internal protests against the Iranian regime (Iran International, January 13, 2026: At least 12,000 killed in Iran crackdown during internet blockade | Iran International). Although Reza Pahlavi, the eldest son of Iran's last shah, is the main opposition leader in exile advocating the return of secular democracy in Iran from his political base in the US, his popularity in Iran remains untested. His late father led a corrupt regime that led to Ayatollah Khomeini's revolutionary victory in 1979. As such, regime change in today's Iran remains difficult, politically speaking. The US and Israeli attack on Iran has galvanized even Revolutionary Guard supporters and possibly made the current regime even stronger.
In conclusion, the US and Israeli war against Iran is expected to be short-lived, ending as quickly as possible and at the latest before the US mid-term elections. Current developments demand the imperative need for self-restraint on the part of all sides, including the US, Israel and Iran. There is a lack of mediators in the process of resuming negotiations, and perhaps some countries in the Middle East can and should intervene decisively before the war can take a worse turn. After all, the lack of political trust between nations and states is the main factor driving the current US and Israeli war against Iran. Unless some degree of trust is restored and rebuilt, the US and Israeli war against Iran could be prolonged, with retaliations from Iran and its allies in the Middle East. From the perspective of international arms control, the point of contention remains the deep political distrust between the US and Israel, on the one hand, and Iran, on the other. Although the war is likely to be limited, characterized by aerial bombardment, missile fire, drone strikes, and the fierce struggle for control of the Strait of Hormuz, any additional spillovers on Iran's neighbors and any unexpected military participation by third parties outside of Central Asia and the Middle East would have potentially disastrous consequences for world peace. Let us hope that the US and Israeli war against Iran will be limited, without excessively adverse impacts on oil prices, which are already rising globally. But unfortunately, the war's damaging impact on relations between the US, Israel and Iran is likely to be long-term, characterized by intermittent conflicts, guerrilla attacks and sudden retaliatory moves – a scenario that points to the likelihood that the US-Israeli military alliance may have already fallen into an oily but deadly quagmire.

item.title: Deputy suggests using television and cinema to “tell Macau’s patriotic stories well”
item.description: Deputy Chan Lai Kei used his speech before the agenda at yesterday's plenary meeting of the Legislative Assembly (AL) to call for the strengthening of “patriotic education through cinema, cultural tourism and diversified studies”. “When we talk about patriotic education, it cannot be limited to school textbooks, as it has to enter community neighborhoods, be on the big screen and go to Hengqin”, said the deputy linked to the Fujian community, adding: “We must transform patriotism into development opportunities for young people, so that the love for the country and for Macau becomes a visible, tangible and perceptible force for Macau”. Chan Lai Kei proposed that cinema and television should be used to “tell the patriotic stories of Macau that are not found in school textbooks”. “I suggest that the Government create specific financial support to encourage more projects with patriotic themes to film in Macau, with the requirement for greater participation from local film and television companies”, said the deputy, explaining that “this would not only contribute to reinforcing the population's sense of belonging to the homeland, but also provide practical opportunities for Macau's young people in the areas of performing arts and film production, supporting them to explore Macau's unique patriotic historical themes, thus transforming patriotic education into opportunities for youth employment and industrial development”. Secondly, Chan urged authorities to make it easier for patriotic education to enter the community. Finally, the deputy called for “good use” of Hengqin to create a “Hengqin-Macau education base”. "Patriotism is not a mere motto, but rather a responsibility and an inheritance of each generation. United by this goal, we must ingrain in the hearts of Macau's young people the love for the country and Macau, integrating patriotic education into their personal and professional development, and into the Hengqin-Macau connection", he argued.

item.title: Investments bring Timor-Leste closer to China, says study
item.description: A recent study highlights how Timor-Leste has sought more investment from China as a strategy for economic development and overcoming geopolitical isolation. The work carried out by William Vogt, senior executive policy fellow at think-tank The Digital Economist, and Guilan Massoud-Moghaddam and Robert Miles Chong of Georgetown University, explores China's growing presence in Timor-Leste and the impact of this relationship on the country's technological development. “The Chinese presence in Timor-Leste occurs at a time when three other nations — India, Australia and Portugal — provide help in information and communication technologies, with Australia being the most active contributor,” highlighted the study. According to the authors, China's technological foreign policy approach is designed to challenge Australian geopolitical interests in Southeast Asia by investing heavily in areas essential to Timorese digital development, including electrification and connectivity. Timor-Leste has sought to fully integrate into the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN), although it faces scrutiny due to economic weaknesses, with “around 42% of Timorese living below the poverty line and the country's sovereign wealth fund in decline”. Accession, diplomatically supported by Beijing, would open access to the ASEAN-China cooperation fund and “tangible economic benefits”, with researchers describing that Timor-Leste may be recalibrating its foreign policy, favoring new partnerships. For the authors, China's growing presence in key sectors — telecommunications, energy, agriculture and defense — suggests that Dili seeks to overcome geopolitical isolation and build a functional digital economy. “China is well positioned to compete by offering the work of national champions such as Huawei, together with other energy and technology suppliers”, they highlighted. The company China Nuclear Industry 22nd Construction Company, for example, was awarded a contract worth 360 million dollars to improve the Timorese national electrical grid, while the technological giant Huawei asserts itself as a central supplier of Internet infrastructure. Australia continues to be a relevant partner, financing fiber optic cables that replace Timorese's dependence on satellite connections, however Beijing has advanced in sensitive areas, supporting Radio and Television of Timor-Leste in the digitalization of content and promoting a digital terrestrial television transmission system. “China increases Timorese access to these broadcasts, creating a potential destination for Chinese propaganda,” warn Vogt, Massoud-Moghaddam and Chong. The study also describes that Sino-Timorese cooperation is not limited to the civil sector, as in July 2024 the two countries agreed to reinforce military exchanges and invest in areas such as personnel training, equipment technology and joint exercises. For the authors, this approach reflects a significant change in Timorese foreign policy, with President José Ramos-Horta increasingly aligning himself with Chinese interests. “Dili can be persuaded to continue to deepen its relations with Beijing as a strategy for continued economic development and overcoming geopolitical isolation,” the study added. In addition to supporting the Timorese Navy and integrating Timor-Leste into its Maritime Silk Road, Beijing has invested in agricultural technology, introducing hybrid rice and methods such as Juncao technology, applied in agricultural areas with digital support. According to the study, financing is often channeled through the China Exim Bank, allowing Dili to access credit for public works and digital projects. A partnership is seen as an alternative to internal capital limitations, dependent on revenue from natural resources. At the same time, e-commerce is seen as a path to modern economic development and Dili could reap rewards by integrating into the regional digital market, using Chinese platforms and products. Lusa

item.title: Has the dynamic in the war between Israel and the US against Iran, which is worsening, changed?
item.description:
On March 14, Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu's war against Iran entered its third week, with no end in sight. Since the initial Israeli and US airstrike on February 28 against Tehran and other cities, the war has only intensified and expanded. It is now a regional conflict that involves the entire Middle East and has had a huge global impact. During the first week of the war, the initiative clearly belonged to the United States and Israel. Its air forces were able to bomb targets and launch missiles with little opposition. The Iranian air force and navy were effectively annihilated. Specialized Israeli intelligence allowed them to precisely locate Iranian missile sites, leaders' residences and hideouts, as well as military bases. Trump and Netanyahu, along with their small circle of advisers, believed that the war would end successfully in a matter of a few weeks. They believed they would then deal with a compliant Iranian regime that would obediently hand over its stockpiles of enriched uranium. This victory did not happen. Instead, many observers believe that the momentum of the war has shifted to the Iranian side. Why was there this change in momentum and what does it portend for the future of this war? What is the impact of the war on the region, Asia and the world? How long will this war last? This war is an illegal war of aggression by two strong men — Benjamin Netanyahu and Donald Trump, who entered the war on a whim, without much thought and without any planning. War violates the United Nations Charter, international law and the Geneva Convention. Quite simply, under the United Nations Charter, which all belligerent parties have signed, it is illegal for a member state to invade another member state unless the latter is facing an imminent attack. This point needs to be emphasized because all of the actions, deaths, injuries and destruction resulting from this illegal war are themselves illegal, and the people involved — Trump, Netanyahu, Defense Ministers Pete Hegseth and Israel Katz — must be held accountable — criminally and financially. Several factors caused the shift in dynamics from the US and Israeli side to the Iranian side. When the United States and Israel beheaded the leadership of the Iranian regime, including its Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the murders included people Donald Trump claimed he could “deal with.” Iran then returned to its long-planned decentralized mosaic defense, essentially spreading its command and control centers across numerous local centers so that local leaders could act on their own. The goal of this mosaic defense is to keep the system in combat even if senior leadership, communications, and key centers are hit. The regime then employed escalatory violence tactics, also called the “escalation trap”. These escalatory tactics are dangerous and could lead to a worldwide conflagration. On Wednesday, Israel attacked Iran's giant South Pars gas field, marking a huge escalation in the war. Iran immediately retaliated by bombing gas fields in Qatar and Saudi Arabia, setting them on fire. For the first time, energy infrastructure has been attacked, which could take years to repair and cause enormous ecological damage. Historians note that during the summer of 1914, European nations employed a series of escalating demands, which allowed the assassination of an Archduke to degenerate into a World War in the space of 6 weeks. Secondly, the Iranians brilliantly employed asymmetric warfare. Essentially, as Prof. Peter Neumann of Kings College London, Iran played its cards badly, but successfully. Asymmetric warfare is a type of warfare between belligerents whose relative military strength, strategy, or power differ significantly. In other words, how could Iran, a regional power, defeat the two greatest military powers in the world and the Middle East — the United States and Israel? To this end, Iran has hit US allies in the Gulf hard. Iran has launched missiles and, more effectively, swarms of drones to target the Gulf States of Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates, Oman, Iraq and Saudi Arabia, initially targeting US military and civilian bases, embassies, businesses and data centers. Because they house these bases, Iran has attacked airports, hotels, homes, port facilities, shopping centers and, recently, oil storage depots and refineries in the Gulf States. Although most missiles and drones have been intercepted, Gulf states fear they will be left without essential interceptors. These tactics have led to the departure of hundreds of thousands of foreign workers, significantly affected air transport around the world and destroyed the reputation that the Gulf States had as a region of calm, luxury and wealth where people can come and do business safely. American tourists and Gulf residents are furious that their government has left them stranded without any evacuation plan. Iran took full advantage of Donald Trump's miscalculations. Trump entered this illegal war without any war plan, strategy, objectives or outcome. Netanyahu and Trump ignored all intelligence reports that the Iranian regime is resilient and unlikely to fall in the wake of the Supreme Leader's assassination. Reports also stated that the opposition is huge but unarmed; and despite its cruelty and brutality, the regime still enjoys considerable and deep support. Trump also ignored the advice of his handpicked chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, General Dan Caine, that if attacked, Iran would close the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's biggest choke points and through which about 20% of the world's oil, liquefied natural gas and fertilizers transit. Furthermore, Iran itself made clear, after the June 2025 Israeli-American attack on its nuclear facilities, that if it were attacked again, it would definitively close the strait. Trump ignored the central importance of the Strait of Hormuz, at enormous cost to himself and the world. Trump now feigns surprise. How could this happen? The closure of the Strait of Hormuz had enormous global ramifications. The prices of oil, natural gas and fertilizers have all increased by around 40 to 50%. These price increases on these essential commodities have caused famine in Africa; a rise in food costs everywhere, fuel and electricity rationing in several Asian countries, or the institution of export bans and price controls. Europe and North America face huge price increases on pump fuel and home heating oil. Global airfares could well double in cost by this summer's tourist season. Trump and Netanyahu's illegal war has caused suffering to hundreds of millions of people around the world, a fact that neither of them cares about in the slightest. It is likely that Russia is providing valuable intelligence reports to the Iranians on the location of US bases and other critical facilities. According to Ukrainian Prime Minister Zelensky, Russia is supplying drones to Iran. This claim is also likely, but Iran already has tens of thousands of its own drones, which cost around $20,000 each. Another victim of the war is the postponement of the long-awaited summit in Beijing between Xi Jinping and Trump. A third victim could well be the postponement of King Charles's visit to the USA next month. The cost of the war is reaching hundreds of billions of US dollars. The US air campaign alone is costing the American public around $1.5 billion a day, money that could be spent on education, housing and healthcare. The Trump regime places a higher priority on killing school-aged girls in Iran than on repairing schools in the United States. Illegal bombings destroyed much more than military targets. In Iran, bombings destroyed houses (about 10,000), banks, energy infrastructure, cultural heritage sites such as the 15th century Golestan Palace and other historical treasures, mosques, hospitals and schools, with the deliberate destruction of a primary school killing around 165 girls aged between 7 and 12. The majority of these attacks constitute war crimes and their perpetrators must be held accountable at the International Criminal Court in The Hague (ICC). Instead of leading the Iranian population to revolt against their hated regime, these bombings caused the Iranian people to hate their supposed “liberators” — the US and the Israelis. On March 8, Israel attacked Tehran's oil storage tanks, setting the skies on fire and providing the world with apocalyptic scenes straight from the Bible. This crime against humanity caused acid rain to fall on the population of Tehran, burning their skin and lungs and causing groundwater and soil pollution, potentially for generations. Now Trump boasts that he might bomb the massive oil storage and export facilities on Kharg Island “just for fun,” which would certainly provoke full-scale Iranian retaliation and likely the destruction of oil fields across the Middle East. Additionally, Iran has suffered more than 1,400 civilian deaths and thousands of injuries, with an estimated two to three million people displaced. Yesterday, Israel assassinated Iran's de facto leader, Ali Larijani, an act that will make a political settlement to the war more difficult, as he was one of the few Iranian officials capable of reaching an agreement. Today, Israel continued the targeted assassination of the head of Iranian intelligence, Esmail Khatib. These assassinations will weaken but not destroy the Iranian regime; they will only make you more determined. In response to Hezbollah's rocket attacks, Israel opened a second front to destroy Hezbollah once and for all. The Israeli Air Force bombed southern Lebanon as well as the Lebanese capital, Beirut. Lebanon, like the Gulf States, is involved in a war it never wanted or caused. The result is billions of dollars in damage in a country that does not have the means to make the necessary repairs, more than 900 deaths and a million people displaced in a tiny country. Iran has made its demands for an end to the war clear: an immediate ceasefire, security guarantees so that no new attacks can occur, and repairs to remedy all damage to its civilian infrastructure. As exigências dos EUA mudam diariamente. Pouco se fala sobre mudança de regime. The Trump regime is now targeting stockpiles of enriched uranium buried beneath a mountain on the outskirts of Isfahan, destroying Iran's ability to produce missiles and launchers, and ending its support for terrorism. Israel wants to destroy Iran, perhaps cause chaos and civil war. Given these demands from all three parties, there is no end to this war, nor what will follow. Israeli policy seeks to destroy the Iranian regime, and if Iran and its people are destroyed, so be it. I fear that this war will last for weeks, months, perhaps years. Trump and Netanyahu are dragging the world into a catastrophe. We are, essentially, passengers on an out-of-control train. Given this absence of a calendar, the world has already changed. The Gulf States may never regain their former safe haven status and may see their economic and financial power decline. Therefore, they are furious with the United States and Donald Trump for destroying their comfortable world by starting this illegal war without their consent. They may have to deal with a weakened but still intact Iranian regime that could demand the withdrawal of all US bases from the Gulf. Europe and Asia realize the insecurity of relying on fossil fuels and will likely move increasingly quickly to manufacture and utilize non-fossil fuel sources. The United States and Donald Trump could face a humiliating failure on the scale of their failures in Afghanistan, Iraq and Vietnam. If the US midterm elections go ahead as scheduled, Trump's MAGA acolytes are likely to face a disastrous defeat in November. The image of the United States around the world is already extremely damaged. The USA is isolated and considered a pariah state. None of the US's traditional allies in Europe and Asia came to Donald Trump's aid. German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius spoke for everyone on Tuesday when he said: “This is not our war. We weren’t the ones who started it.” Finally, in the wake of the war, Iran is likely to do everything in its power to develop an atomic bomb, following North Korea's example. The fatwa that the Supreme Leader imposed against Iran's development of a nuclear bomb ended with his assassination. The world order has once again undergone a profound change, facing a possible economic recession and inflation, or even stagflation.
MichaelShare Professor of Sino-Russian Relations at Hong Kong Baptist University

item.title: Commission begins hearings into worst fire in Hong Kong since 1948
item.description:
An independent investigative committee yesterday began hearings into the deadliest fire in Hong Kong since 1948 and heard testimony highlighting the failures that contributed to the fire killing 168 people.
Former residents and families of victims have been waiting for answers since November, when the fire devastated seven buildings in the Wang Fuk public housing complex, which housed more than 4,600 people. In opening remarks, the commission's main lawyer said that the flames began on a platform in a light well between two apartments, with cigarette butts found in the area and on scaffolding. Victor Dawes pointed out as factors that led to the disaster the disconnected fire alarms and hose systems, the use of non-fire resistant scaffolding networks and the covering of windows with foam sheets. “On the day of the fire, almost all fire safety systems intended to protect lives failed due to human factors,” lamented the lawyer. The fire began on November 26 and quickly consumed seven of the apartment complex's eight buildings. The police detained 22 people on suspicion of voluntary homicide, in addition to another six on suspicion of fraud. Hong Kong's anti-corruption agency also detained 23 people, including consultants, contractors and members of the complex's condominium association. The commission, led by a judge and created in December, will also examine whether there were systemic problems, such as the manipulation of tenders, in maintenance and renovation work on large-scale buildings. The Government of the former British colony stated at the time that the commission's work should take nine months. After hearing opening remarks from lawyers, the commission will hear oral testimony from witnesses, including residents of Wang Fuk. Various government departments are involved in the hearings. Before the hearing, Phyllis Lo, who lost her mother in the fire, said she hopes to hear conclusions from different parties to get a comprehensive understanding of the incident. "We want to know what caused this disaster and who is responsible. At the very least, we want to know what started the fire and why it spread so quickly, to the point of becoming completely uncontrollable," said another resident, Seneca Lee. Many former Wang Fuk residents continue to live in temporary housing spread across the city. In February, Hong Kong authorities proposed buying back the property rights of fire victims, citing the results of a survey into residents' preferences. But the announcement dashed the hopes of some residents, who wanted to rebuild apartments on the site. Hong Kong leader John Lee said on Tuesday that the government is working on plans to allow affected residents to visit apartments and retrieve some of their belongings in April. Lusa

item.title: Deputies call for greater supervision of humanoid robots
item.description:
After the case of the humanoid robot that scared an elderly woman on the street, deputies Chao Ka Chon and Kou Ngon Seng asked the Government to strengthen supervision and regulation of this type of technology.
At yesterday's plenary meeting of the Legislative Assembly (AL), deputies Chao Ka Chon and Kou Ngon Seng called for increased supervision over humanoid robots. Remember that a video recently circulated of an elderly woman who was scared by a humanoid robot on the street and had to be taken to the hospital. The intervention before the agenda by the two nominated deputies then dealt with this technology, starting by pointing out that the 15th National Five-Year Plan proposes the promotion of embodied intelligence, which can take different physical forms, such as humanoid robots, robot dogs or intelligent vehicles, for example. These robots have capabilities for perception, decision-making, action and human-computer interaction, “constituting a fundamental advance for artificial intelligence to move from virtual to real”. “Its application potential is enormous in areas such as scientific education, guided cultural and tourist visits, urban management, community services and commercial actions, contributing to promoting the intelligent transformation of society”, highlighted Chao Ka Chon, noting that, “when encouraging technological innovation and the installation of industries, attention must also be paid to public security and social order”. In the intervention, Chao stated that the case of the elderly woman who was scared by a robot “demonstrates the insufficiency of its supervision and application regulations”. “Humanoid robots have the capacity for autonomous movement and interaction with the environment, but, in the event of a breakdown, algorithmic anomalies or operational errors, they can threaten public safety, resulting in complex problems such as deaths, injuries, property damage and legal responsibilities”, warned the intervention. Os deputados nomeados afirmaram que, “atendendo ao rápido desenvolvimento da inteligência artificial e à diversificação de cenários da sua aplicação, legislar sobre a matéria de forma abrangente leva tempo e é preciso equilibrar a inovação e a segurança”. It was suggested that, “instead of relying solely on legislation, the principle of valuing development and regulation should be observed, while simultaneously ensuring innovation and security, with the adoption of various measures, for example, institutional guidance, records management, risk prevention and control, and awareness raising”, in order to guarantee the safety of citizens and tourists. The deputies asked for instructions to be drawn up to monitor these technologies, “clarifying the boundary between the security inherent to public applications and privacy”. “Specific instructions or standards for the use of humanoid robots and robotic dogs in public places” must also be defined, covering aspects such as the physical safety of citizens, data privacy and “behavioral limits” to “expressly prohibit any operation or exhibition that frightens the public, interferes with traffic or disturbs public order”. On the other hand, parliamentarians suggested the creation of a mandatory insurance purchase regime for these cases. Finally, Chao Ka Chon and Kou Ngon Seng urged the Government to regulate commercial advertising and activities carried out in public using these technologies.

item.title: 91 suicides recorded in 2025. Number is the highest ever in the Macao SAR
item.description:
The number of deaths by suicide has been skyrocketing in recent years. Demographic statistics from the Directorate of Statistics and Census Services (DSEC) show that last year 91 cases of suicide were recorded; ten years ago, the number was at 50. FINAL POINT, the Health Services say that “the causes of suicides are complex”, urging residents to pay more attention to the people around them and to encourage individuals suffering from emotional disturbances to actively seek professional support.
In 2025, 91 cases of suicide were recorded, indicate demographic statistics recently released by the Directorate of Statistics and Census Services (DSEC). This is the highest number ever in the Macao SAR. Demographic statistics show a significant increase in the number of deaths by suicide over the last decade. In 2015, there were 50 suicides, almost half the number in 2025. According to DSEC figures, in 2024 there were 86 deaths from voluntary self-harm. Previously, remember, the Health Services had recorded 90 suicides in 2024. THE FINAL POINT went to the DSEC archive and went back to 2000. From that year until 2015, the number of deaths by suicide fluctuated between 44 and 75. After 2015, the cases gradually increased. In 2016, 60 cases were registered, the following year there were 66 and in 2018 two more. In 2019, there was a drop to 56, but in 2020 the number of suicides shot up to 69. In 2021 it dropped back to 50, however, from then on it continued to rise: 73 cases in 2022; 78 in 2023; 86 in 2024 and finally 91 last year. Until 2024, the Health Services publicly released statistical data on deaths by suicide every quarter, but have since stopped sharing this information.
CAUSES OF SUICIDE “ARE COMPLEX”
Asked by PONTO FINAL about the number of suicides last year, the Health Services indicated that “the causes of suicide are complex”, involving “often mental illnesses, psychological, socioeconomic, family, interpersonal relationship and biogenetic factors”. The authorities emphasize, however, that suicide can be prevented and, “for effective prevention and control, the attention and active participation of everyone is necessary, playing the role of ‘guardian’ of suicide prevention”. To help reduce the incidence of suicides, Health Services advises residents to maintain close contact, communicate more and pay more attention to those around them, and encourage individuals suffering from emotional distress to actively seek professional support. “The Macao SAR Government attaches great importance to the physical and mental health of the population, integrating health elements into all public policies”, emphasize the authorities, noting that, currently, the nine Health Centers in Macau provide mental health services. Furthermore, the Health Services point out, the Government has constantly financed the psychotherapy services provided by the General Union of Residents' Associations of Macau and the Macau Women's Association. In collaboration with the Macau Youth Development Service Centre, the Emotional Support Hotline 28712356 – 24-hour mental health counseling services – was also launched, and the mental health self-assessment tool Emotional Self-Check was made available, “to help residents identify their psychological health status, encouraging those facing emotional difficulties to call, on their own initiative, the Emotional Support Hotline (28712356) or the Hope Hotline of life of Cáritas (28525222), in order to obtain help.

item.title: Amélia António re-elected president of Casa de Portugal in Macau
item.description: Amélia António was re-elected as president of Casa de Portugal in Macau this Wednesday for another new biennial term from 2026-2028. In a note released by this Portuguese-based association in Macau after the elections, Amélia António, on behalf of the Board, expressed gratitude to the members for their continued support for the work of Casa de Portugal de Macau. “On behalf of the Board, I thank the members for their participation in the Electoral Assembly and their support for the work carried out by Casa de Portugal in Macau in the general interest of the community over 25 years”, emphasized Amélia António, who has been at the head of the association since 2005. The members of Casa de Portugal went to the polls on the 18th, elections that again had only one list for the association's managing bodies. Headed by Amélia António, the list also included Ricardo Igreja as vice-president, Diana Soeiro as secretary and Nuno Figueiredo as treasurer. The general assembly board is now made up of João Manuel Costa Antunes, the president, André Ritchie, vice-president, and Filomena McGuire, secretary. The supervisory board includes Armindo Vaz, Paula Silva and Andrea Aleixo. Amélia António, who is also a lawyer and former president of the Superior Advocacy Council of the Macau Lawyers Association, began to be part of the Board of the General Assembly of the Casa de Portugal in 2003, and assumed the role of president of the board since June 2005, succeeding the engineer João Manuel Costa Antunes. This year Casa de Portugal in Macau celebrates its 25th anniversary. Created in 2001, the association aims to promote, disseminate and defend the Portuguese language and culture, the heritage and identity of Macau, as well as disseminating Chinese culture, through exhibitions, shows, festivals, publications, video productions, and artistic teaching carried out at the School of Arts and Crafts.

item.title: “Pessanha’s verses have a cadence that asks to be sung”
item.description:
Centennial poetry digested by algorithms. One hundred years after the death of Camilo Pessanha in Macau, an unprecedented musical project returns his poems to the city that saw them born. Sunk Ships Sing Clepsydra is an album of 30 tracks that set the entirety of Clepsydra, the Symbolist collection from 1920, with lyrics sung in Patuá-inflected Portuguese. The project's author, Ian Watts, explains to PONTO FINAL how the poetry of the “poet of two homelands” found an unexpected echo in Shidaiqu, the Shanghai jazz of the 1930s, and why Patuá, a language on the verge of extinction, is the right voice for this testimony.
The poet who spent his life rewriting the same verses found, a century later, someone who rewrote them in music generated by machines. Camilo Pessanha's life and work have always been linked to music, but now it goes further, in a connection that he himself would never have thought would happen. “The idea was born from recognition, not from invention” says Ian Watts, writer and translator with Portuguese roots in Hawaii and California, who knows Pessanha's work deeply. It was at the end of the nineties that he came across the legendary poet's manuscripts in the National Library of Lisbon. Among them, a replica of the Black Notebook. He saw the amendments, the drafts, the verses that Pessanha continued to rewrite long after he had considered them finished. The project he now presents, with the evocative name Sunk Ships Sing Clepsydra, is an album of 30 tracks that transform the entirety of Clepsydra into music. The songs are sung in a Portuguese that naturally transfers to Patuá, the romantic Creole that encompassed the symbolic daily life of Pessanha in Macau. The chosen “genre” is Shidaiqu, a sound mix that flourished in Shanghai in the 1930s, where traditional Chinese melodies fused with the harmony and instrumentation of the recently popularized American Jazz. The collection of songs, available for free on the Sunk Ships website and Spotify, is built on a deliberate production concept. A Macanese band in Shanghai. The singer, Mica, is Macanese and the recording has warmth, immersive, which makes the listener feel like they are inside a room, with the reverberation doing the work that the rhythm section would do in another context. However, this band is entirely fictional, a product of imagination and technology. The album was produced using artificial intelligence (AI) music generation tools, which allowed a single person to create something that would otherwise have required a period ensemble, a studio and considerable resources. Ian Watts describes the process as a collaboration where the AI ​​came in as the instrumentalist and he as the editor, constantly resisting the machine's tendency towards the “polished and the beautiful”, in search of something that sounds like the memory of music.
How did the idea of ​​taking Pessanha’s entire “Clepsidra” and putting it to music now, a hundred years after the poet’s death, come about? I've known Pessanha's work for a long time. At the end of the nineties I consulted his manuscripts at the National Library in Lisbon — and among them was a replica of the Caderno Negro. I saw the amendments, the erasures, the verses that he continued to rewrite long after he had written them. What stayed with me was not only the literary quality, but the strangeness of his situation: a man completely absorbed by Chinese culture, writing in Portuguese about this dissolution, in a city that was both Portuguese and not at the same time. When I started thinking about musicalizing Clepsidra, the connection to Shanghai shidaiqu wasn't an idea — it was a recognition. The two traditions occupy the same emotional space. It arose from a kind of coincidence between time and text. I was reading Clepsidra and realized that the poems already had music within them — not metaphorically, but structurally. Pessanha's verses have a cadence that demands to be sung, and the shidaiqu of the 30s has exactly the quality that the poems need: that mix of elegance and imminent collapse. Pessanha's Macau and Zhou Xuan's Shanghai are sister cities in this sense — two colonial outposts where modernity arrived poisoned with melancholy. The centenary of death was a pretext, not a reason.
We know that the book was published without Pessanha knowing, somewhat backwards. Was musicalizing the 30 poems in their original order a way of giving coherence to a book published without the poet's participation? That's part of the answer, but The Black Notebook complicates it in an interesting way. Pessanha was no stranger to the process — he was a compulsive reviewer. The problem was the distance and the illness, not the lack of interest. The manuscripts show a man who continued to work on poems after they were published, moving verses around, testing whether the sentiment was in the right place. Musicalizing the book in its order was, in this sense, less a compensation for his absence and more a gesture analogous to what he himself did: taking the texts and discovering what becomes of them when you handle them again.
After delving into the work, in your opinion, what does Shanghai's Shidaiqu have in common with Pessanha's symbolist and decadent poetry? Both work with dissolution. Classical Shidaiqu — the songs of Zhou Xuan, of Li Xianglan — is not happy music with oriental instrumentation. It's music about things that slip: the love that has passed, the city that will change, the self that is being lost. Pessanha's symbolism is not literature about ideas — it is literature about sensations that are already falling apart as they are felt. Both genres exist at the moment when something has already been, but is not yet completely over. It is in this interval that the album lives.
Patuá is almost a character on this album. For those who don't know, what does this language bring to poems? Is there any word or expression that has become especially powerful, that in Portuguese would not have the same weight? It is important to say that it is not pure patuá, as you hear in Doci Papiaçám di Macau or read in Adé. And that was a deliberate decision, not a limitation. What I saw in Macau — and what anyone who has spent time with the Macanese community recognizes — is that language does not function in watertight compartments. People slip between Portuguese, Patuá and Cantonese within the same conversation, sometimes within the same sentence, without this being marked as a change. It's the natural way of speaking for someone who grew up between several languages ​​and doesn't have to choose. That's what I tried to replicate in the lyrics. Some poems are closer to Portuguese, others closer to Patuá, and within each song there are moments in which the singer slides from one register to the other. The singer is Macanese, she was in Shanghai in the 1930s, she is multilingual — and what she does with Pessanha's verses is what anyone in that situation would do: she uses the language that suits the moment, and doesn't explain herself. What the patuá brings, when it enters, is not local color or ornament. It brings fragility. It's a different way in which time and comparison work in the sentence. In Patuá, it marks a complete action in a more definitive way than in Portuguese — when she sings, the apple orchard is already in bloom, it already weighs differently. And tancu, the particle of comparison, does not have the formal weight of Pessanha's literary how — “tancu um luz se erased” says the same thing, but without architecture, as if it were simply true and not a poetic construction. The decision not to fix everything in Patuá was also a decision of honesty: this is how the language survived, mixed, adapted, slipping in and out of Portuguese. To put it in pure amber would be to falsify it. And there was something that interested me in this idea: that Patuá can continue to be reimagined, to appear in new contexts, to be heard in ways that its original speakers did not plan. Even though the band is imaginary and Shanghai is 1933, the language is doing something real. It is not being preserved. It is being used.
“Clepsidra” is a book about time running away, about dissolution, about how we are losing ourselves. How do you translate that into music without being obvious? Not through conventional dynamics — it's not an album that builds to a climax and then resolves. It's more about texture and density: the first songs have a certain space around the voices, a deceptive lightness. Halfway through, that space begins to close. The final themes are heavier, slower, as if time itself were becoming viscous. There is no stated emotional peak — there is a point where the music simply stops trying to escape. The last poem ends with an instruction — do not breathe — and the album treats this literally.
How did you manage to prevent technology from stealing the soul of the project? How far has AI helped and where did you come in? He stole a few times, and I had to throw away what I had stolen. AI tends toward the polished, toward the beautiful, toward what sounds like music. What I wanted was what sounds like music memory — the difference between a photograph and a faded photograph that someone kept in a box. The work was mainly one of resistance: resisting clean versions, resisting well-tuned voices, resisting arrangements that sounded like contemporary production pretending to be old. AI came in as an instrumentalist. I came in as an editor, and as the person who had been with Pessanha's manuscripts and knew what he didn't want.
Patuá is disappearing, few people speak it. By making 30 songs in this language, do you feel that this project is also a way of keeping it alive? I don't think of it as preservation—that word implies a tongue still, fixed, guarded against decay. What interested me was different: showing that patuá can be reimagined. That it can appear in a context that its native speakers never anticipated — Portuguese symbolist poetry sung in Shanghai jazz — and still sound like itself. Not a file, but a tongue that still has the ability to move. What I heard in Macau was a living language precisely because it was fluid, because its speakers did not treat it as a relic. This album tries to honor that: patuá comes and goes, mixes with Portuguese, does what it has always done. The band is imaginary and Shanghai is 1933, but the language is really working. But there is also something honest about using a language that is disappearing to set to music a poet who spent his life writing about things that die. If the album makes someone try to find out what patuá is, or listen to a recording of the last people who speak it naturally, it's already worth it. It is not a file. It's a testimony.
Can you imagine Pessanha, in his Macau, listening to the album? Listening to his own poems sung in Creole, with Shanghai jazz… what would he think? I think I would be disconcerted for the first thirty seconds. But Pessanha was a man who realized, probably later than he should have, that Macau had changed him in ways he couldn't undo — and that this wasn't necessarily a loss. In the manuscripts you can see someone who continued to work on the poems, looking for the right place for each feeling. Hearing his own verses sung in a language he knew but never treated as a literary language, with music from a neighboring town he might never have visited — I think he would recognize something. That the poems were never entirely yours. That they belonged there.
